What sets the HP Gland to go off its regular schedule from 1x a day?
Barometric Pressure from one of the following  causes but not limited to:
Change in season - lengthening of days Spring to Summer, Summer to Fall, Fall to Winter
Geographical location - by the coast you get lots of low/high pressure changes from storms or marine layer or fog
Weather changes - low pressure from storms (high pressure is usually good as it is stable)
Stress - adds up to pressure in the head which affects the HP
irregular sleeping patterns
stimulants
Why is it important to keep it on a regular schedule?
When it goes off from the body's clock, trouble starts for CH people.
For example let's say I have four CH per day like clockwork at
11am
4pm
11pm
4am
this is HP (Hypothalamus Gland) triggering at the wrong time
Here's what I think is going on...
At 11am the HP is triggered for the second time today. It is off the from the body's clock. They should be in sync going off at the same time. This would be my normal waking time.  We all have one natural rising time, but two different alarms in the form of an HP clock and Body clock. Hopefully they go off once a day, together in sync.
My HP clock is operating too fast
I think that as opposed to a trigger, the headache has come about because the HP clock has been "Speed Up".  The change in seasons confused it and now it is running to fast. HP clock thinks a day is only six hours long instead of 24. It is moving too fast.
Now the HP gland is triggering at 11 rather than at my natural time is because it is off.  In my case it is already on the second alarm in a single day! My body is getting the message to wake up, its the start of the day again. The rest of the body realizes it is already almost mid day. The body clock is working fine. It reacts to the message and says "terrorist" and sends its defenses to attack. Instantly the histamines flow and everything gets worse now. Chemical imbalances in the body. Pressure builds in your sinuses. This sinus pressure puts pressure on and triggers the TG nerve. Now the pain receptors have been flooded by a "key " molecule that fits into them. This molecule we will call K. It is the molecule that fits in the receptor causing a protein P to be released in the brain saying "OW! HOLY HELL THIS HURTS!"
Your body clock does not quit fighting. It continues to send messages and fight the imaginary terrorist trying to wake up the body for no reason.
Your brain is flooded by K everywhere. all your pain receptors are being blown up and sucked out of the protein P.
You feel the headache as long as K is filled and triggering P to your brain.
The good news is P runs out. That is when your headache stops. Some people hold a lot of P some hold a little. This is called tolerance of Pain. you have a  lot of P you will suffer a lot of pain. You have a little P, you will have pain but shorter headaches. I have a lot of P. My headaches can be minimum 2-4 hours and then 2-4 more sitting around as the P leav

Couldn't be more relieved to be writing this post, but I'm delighted to report that I'm 9 days pain free, so I am feeling quite confident that the beast is back in the box for the foreseeable. I wasn't able to find a huge number of detailed bust reports for LSD, so I wanted to write up a report that was quite thorough, in case it's useful to someone (even if it's just future me!)
Context: I'm 27, male, based in London. This is my third cycle so far, so I'm still very new to ECH. My first two cycles were two years apart. In my first cycle, I received a diagnosis after 6 weeks, found Custerbusters after 8 weeks, and spent the following 2 weeks on a slightly manic LSD binge, after being quite desperate for some relief. The cycle ended after that, unsurprisingly, though I was never sure if it was the L or the cycle ending naturally.
After two years pain free, it returned in 2017. In that cycle, I only had access to MM. It took 5 weeks to break it, but got there in the end. I then had 2 and a half years pain free before my current cycle, which followed a missed maintenance dose and an errant glass of wine in June.
Day 1: My cycle with a relatively manageable K5. I went for a relatively modest ~40ug LSD, mostly sub-hallucinogenic, in accordance with the regimen as it stood in 2015. I experienced mild slapbacks (a pressure building in my forehead) as the effects wore off. In the days that followed, my attacks remained consistent in terms of pain, with frequency staying at/below 1 per day.
Day 5: I got hit with a K8, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. I dosed again afterwards, around ~50ug. When I came down, I was hit with a K8 slapback, and then a K9 the following day. Frequency was now up to around 2-3/day, often with one attack significantly worse than others.
Day 9: Dosed again, this time ~100ug, after coming across more up to date advice which suggested 'recreational doses' for busting with L. I decided to bring the bust forward a day, basically because I had to be functional the next day, but also because in my recreational use I'd found that my tolerance level was usually unnoticeable after 3 days off. Perhaps this was a bad call though, as I got hit with 2 and a half hours of K10, hands down the worst attack of my life. Decided after this that I needed to get hold of some O2, as the caffeine and hot showers weren't particularly helpful anymore.
Day 12: Finally was able to start the D3 regimen. Also started cutting chocolate from my diet and stopped skipping meals, as I began to suspect these were triggers.
Day 16: After several days of battling my doctor for it, I finally got my hands on some O2. Can't believe I went without it for the past 16 days, and the two cycles before this one! I felt giddy with relief after killing my first attack with it, made a mental note to ensure I had it from day 1 of my next cycle.
Day 17: Dosed again with around~100ug LSD, very slapback heavy trip but super manageable with my tank by my side.

HI all,
Long time viewer here, but first time posting.
I have had CH since 21, I'm 33 now and was diagnosed just last year.
Over the years I've seen dentists, optometrists, an ENT specialist, an allergist It wasnt until I visited the emergency room during a cycle in 2016 that a doctor gave me oxygen (attack disappeared) and told me "you have cluster headaches". Finally saw a neurologist who diagnosed me. I've had  the MRI and CT scan the whole jazz.
my cycles generally happen around daylight savings in March or around the first day of Spring, however the last 2 cycles I have had (2018 and 2019) were started from sudden weather changes.
In 2018 it was a day in January where we saw the temperature shoot up to 15 degrees Celsius out of nowhere, and this July it was when the temperature dropped to 15 degrees Celsius out of nowhere.
My 2018 cycle was the longest i have ever had at 10 weeks, usually they stick around for 5-6 weeks. I am always afraid that 10 weeks will become the new trend.
My neurologist put me on Topamax this year to which i saw small relief, it did bring them down from 4 a day to 2 a day. I did some reading and found that changing my diet to sugar free carb free while on Topamax gave me complete pain free status. I could even smoke cannabis which is usually a huge trigger for me while in cycle. The only problem with this was, I dropped 20-25 lbs since July 13. It was the first time I could see physically how this disease has affected me and it brought me to very dark places mentally.
Last tuesday I decided to give busting a try, I dont have any reservations towards psychedelics I just havent done any of them since highschool and was nervous. So I came off the topamax on Tuesday and waited the 5 days. I started the D3 regimen and was getting attacks daily, aborted by energy drinks.  On sunday I dropped a gram of MM and boy was it ever intense.Felt like I was putting my brain in a very tight high ponytail LOL. On Monday morning I received a visit from the beast. Yesterday.....nothing....smoked a dooby.....nothing. This Morning nothing. although I will dose again this Friday I truelly believe I have broken the cycle and I cannot say how elated I am.
I want to extend my thanks to this forum ...although i am always skeptic I believe I will continue with the D3 regimen and busting going forward.
My question  .... I am in Canada and for some reason I can only find D3 in 1,000 IU. Meaning i have been taking 10 a day which i dont mind for now but I know will become tiresome and throw me out of the loop going forward. How do I obtain at least a 5,000 IU of this magic?
secondly, I take the vitamin K2, Omega 3 fish oil, Magnesium and the D3...benadryl in the day and melatonin at night  and have seen success is it necessary to add the others?
AGAIN, I want to thank you all...your work here is no comparison to any doctor I have seen in the past years. and although you might not see it...people are watching, reading, taking advice and finding comf

21 year Episodic. Clusters used to happen every 9 months, lasted 1 month with 1 or two attack / day lasting 1 hour each. The cycle would be like clockwork, almost to the day. Back then I was treating with maxalt wafers only, and running cold water on my head. Eventually was prescribed amitriptyline, which unsurprisingly didn't do anything. Sometimes my cycle would
end
with a day-long attack. Different from a regular cluster headache, but, still on the cluster side. Really weird. Anyway, I would often end up in the ER with these ending attacks, but, they were always bad news / good news. Bad because I knew they would last at least 24 hours; and were brutal (not as bad as a CH attack, but, pretty unbearable due to the length of the attack and no sleep). Good because I knew the cycle would end after that one weird one. Eventually saw my first neurologist in 2012 after a trip to the ER (10 years after my CHs started). I've had all of the required brain scans, etc. and got the official diagnosis. Neurologist recommended maxalt wafers, home oxygen, prednisone taper at the start of a cycle, and verapamil for the duration of the cycle and for 6 weeks after it clears. Family doctor wouldn't prescribe me any of those things except for Maxalt. Said everything else was too risky.
Found a new Family Doctor 2 years later (2014). Got me hooked up with another Neurologist, and got the same treatment plan recommended. Got all of my scripts filled, etc.
Over the years, my cycles spread out longer (longer remission), but, more attacks while in cycle. One cycle, I didn't realize the risks of too many triptans (was taking 2, 3, sometimes 4 per day that cycle). Ended up getting really bad rebound headaches. Cycle lasted much longer, and I ended up getting upwards of 8 attacks per day. After that cycle, and realizing what had happened; I've tried to avoid the triptans whenever possible. I rely on home o2, and only use a maxalt nasal spray if I'm out and don't have access to o2 or any other options.
Started the D3 regimen in 2019, and have been taking all of the cofactors and everything daily since then.
For the last few cycles, my treatment plan has been:
1) 13 day prednisone taper at the onset of a cycle; 60mg 2 days, 50mg 3 days, 40mg 3 days, 30mg 2 days, 20mg 2 days, 10mg 1 day
2) Verapamil - 120mg x 3 / day
3) Home o2 - 25lpm through Cluster o2 mask (coupled with the odd sip of really cold ice water through a straw)
4) Rizatriptan Nasal spray only for emergencies (5mg)
5) Still doing the D3 regimen
6) Have tried adding melatonin in before bed (10mg)
7) Energy drinks to try to help clear shadows
8) Ice-pack on the head for shadows, and sometimes during attacks (other times I can't stand it)
9) No alcohol
The prednisone taper has killed 2 cycles right at the start. One cycle it didn't completely kill it (the cycle above with the triptan overuse); and I ended up doing a 2nd prednisone taper, which did end it

I sat on this post for 24 hours thinking it's too much and it's way too long. But there's something inside me still saying to share it for whoever it might touch, so here it goes...
Hello Everyone,
(*This turned into a book as I was writing it as I really allowed my passion to flow. Thank you to anyone willing to take the time to read it!*)
I am the mother of a 34 year old son who was diagnosed with CH when he was about 20. He wasn’t living with me at the time and when he spoke to me about his headaches he called them migraines so I only became aware of the actual diagnosis of Cluster Headaches and what that truly means recently. Since then I have been researching everything I can find on CH in my attempt to help him. This site has been invaluable for all of the information it contains as well as for everyone’s heartfelt sharing of their own experiences with CH. I am so appreciative!
While reading through the forums the other evening I had a major AHA moment as I started connecting the dots with CH and my son’s past medical history. As children he and his 2 years older sister constantly had strep throat and ear infections and were continuously on amoxicillin. Their younger brother somehow managed to avoid getting sick in a similar fashion. My husband died when my children were 4, 7 and 9 years old and losing the father they adored obviously impacted each of my children deeply, as it did me as well. 3 months after his death both of my 2 older kids underwent tonsillectomies since the strep infections were constant and amoxicillin was no longer keeping the infections at bay.
My daughter went on to become much healthier after the surgery, my son did not. While he stopped getting the strep throats that had plagued him for so long, he started getting constant stomach aches. Within a year he was sick so much of the time that he had missed over 1/3 of the school year. Our family practice doctors were at a loss to explain what was going on or diagnose him so referred him to a pediatric gastroenterologist who eventually diagnosed him with Crohn’s disease, a form of IBD (Inflammatory Bowel Disease) after tissue biopsy showed severe inflammation of parts of his bowel. The problem was that nothing Western Medicine had to offer him helped or alleviated his pain. Even prednisone didn’t seem to make much difference, all while he blew up like a balloon and suffered all the side effects that go along with steroids.
At this time I met a woman who happened to work for a Naturopath and she suggested that I take my son to see her. I had been working as a registered nurse for years so I was deeply steeped in allopathic medicine and hesitant to try something that wasn’t proven or necessarily approved by the medical community. Fortunately our family practice doctors were wonderful and encouraged me to take my son to see her. They were extremely frustrated that they hadn’t been able to help him and felt that I might learn something new that I could report back to them abo

Hello Everyone,
I’ve been an episodic cluster headache sufferer for nearly twenty years now. I am father of three with a fairly stressful job in the tech industry. I usually get a flare up of cluster headaches every 12 to 18 months. They typically last 3 months.
Last winter, the headaches were starting to come on, and I did 4 doses of magic mushrooms one week apart (around 1 gram each time). It did the trick and I experienced nearly four months of completely pain free existence, not even a hint of shadows, which is very unusual for me.
Unfortunately in the spring the headaches came back. I only had 1 gram of mushrooms left and it wasn’t enough. I never experienced a flare up in the spring so I thought I was safe for a while. I was about to make another batch… Lesson learned.
I’m now 3 and a half months into this flare up. It is starting to cool off finally. In the middle of the summer I was at 800 mg of verapamil. The side effects weren’t pleasant. Now I’m down to 480. I plan on going fully verapamil free in the next three weeks. Five days after being off verapamil, I plan on taking 2 grams of mushroom as a precaution, and repeat the dose every two or three weeks for the foreseeable future.
I want to thank Bob Wold and the people behind cluster busters. The site and forums is an invaluable resource for cluster headaches sufferers. Over the past several years I visited the site over a dozen times to seek out information. Sometimes I won’t visit cluster busters for years (perhaps as a way to compartmentalize this terrible condition) only to come back when the headaches return.
I have several questions, mostly about drugs interactions when busting. I’ve posted a few of these in the past. And I’ve search the forums for answers, but there is so many search results that it can be difficult to find out the most agreed upon answer.
Is Benadryl safe when busting?
There seems to be conflicting anecdotes in the forums. Is there any consensus on this point? I’ve noticed that it helps with nighttime shadows/attacks. Should I clear my system of it for five days first?
Does CBD oil affect the busting process?
I’m curious about the effects of CBD oil on reducing shadows. I haven’t tried it yet. I’ll wait until after
busting, but I’m curious if there are any negative consequences when busting.
Are high doses of melatonin safe when busting?
I’m currently taking 18mg. I usually lower it to 3 mg when I’m not experiencing cluster headaches but I’m afraid of lowering until after the bust. Melatonin really helps my nocturnal attacks and shadows.
Is anybody else greatly affected by Sunlight?
The hardest aspect of being a cluster headache sufferer is that I’m basically allergic to sunlight. Exposing myself to the sun during a flareup will make the nocturnal attacks impossible to stop even with oxygen. Outside of a flareup, it gives me very strong shadows, and it can more than anything else trigger a new flare up. Going down south in the winter, or spending a couple of da

Hi everyone, CH's are new to me, I am in my mid 60's and have never had any headache issues in the past. I was devastated when this hit me late last October, I thought I was going to die. I went to a hospital, private doctor, ENT and then a different hospital where a doctor finally suggested that I may be having cluster headaches. My symptoms are ~1 1/2 hrs. after going to sleep followed by a 5-10 minute onset. Then the worst pain imaginable for 15-20 minutes. The episodes would repeat if I tried to sleep again day or night. Anyways I wanted to tell everyone what seems to work for me. When I found this site and read about CB, someone whipped up a liquid suspension.
I was concerned about the you know what side effect, but I was told I would have to consume all of it for that. During the following treatment below, I never experienced that effect. Tasted like citrus was in my mouth about 1 minute swished around and under the tongue. No upset stomach or any side effects that I noticed. I don't know what the ratio of mg to ml is or the method it was produced but I guess I could find out.

Day 6: At bedtime I took 2.5

Day 8: Appointment with neurologist what a joke but that's another story. I am stopping the melatonin tonight. I am sure the Bstr dose will be 5ml but I would like to stop taking it now, but I am afraid of a relapse. You know how that feels!
I am thinking of weening my way down similar to the way I started. Any suggestions?
I also had a dose of positive encouragement from my wonderful wife who nagged me to death that I was going to get better.
I know this is not the suggested method as mentioned at the website, but this is working for me, and I hope it might help someone else.

This may seem like a strange and random and lengthy missive, and IÂ’m sorry for that.  Who knows what parts of it may be pertinent?

So let me begin by sharing some vitals:
DOB:  8/1973
Gender:  Male
Weight: 130
Height: 5Â’8
I am a smoker (about a pack a day, give or take, since 1994Â—yes, I know this is a nasty, nasty thing and I need to quit.)
I do not drink alcohol except rarely (maybe twice a year) socially.
I have no serious health conditions and have never had any kind of major surgery.
Currently, IÂ’m undergoing treatment for a spring headache cycle.  (I have two a year:  Fall cycle begins mid-October and ends sometime in December.  Spring cycle begins early to mid-March and ends in late May.)
Details of current treatment:
1)      Nerve block/steroid injection administered on 3/31/11 in the right side of the back of my neck.  (Maybe the vagus nerve?  My neurologist told me what nerve it was, but I canÂ’t remember.  Occipital?  Someone probably has an idea based on the limited information IÂ’m able to provide.)

My last cycle was nearly 7 years ago and that was by far the longest period between cycles in over 25 years. I have been in cycle for nearly a month and full blown for nearly two weeks. I actually thought I had out grown my headaches (I know wishful thinking) until this last cycle. I have oxygen and imitrex (which I hate doing) and am intrigued by the psilocybin approach not to mention desperate. Sorry for the long story but here's my two questions. 1. has anyone else had any success with melatonin. 2. Do I have to quit taking melatonin if I decide to try psilocybin approach. text:
First off, I want to say that it's a life-saver that this community exists. This is my first post, and I figured I'd begin by sharing my busting story.
At the age of 29, I have been a cluster headache sufferer for 7 years. My attacks are yearly, at the end of the summer, generally lasting around two months. September and October are my cluster months. I am currently in a cluster phase.
Last year was my first year of being able to acquire the necessary medicine to attempt cluster busting. My approach may have been somewhat unorthodox, but the results were miraculous.
In 2012, my first (and only) cluster headache came late in the evening. I ate a modest dose of mushrooms at the onset of the attack. This was enough to receive a mild hallucinogenic effect, so I would estimate that it was around a gram and a half consumed. Eating these mushrooms did not abort that individual headache. I recall laying in bed in severe pain for several hours until the headache finally passed and I was able to sleep.
The next day, I began taking a small dose of LSD early in the day. Since LSD and Psilocin both have a tendency to prevent sleep, and since cluster headaches seem to be tied with the hypothalamus and circadian rhythms, I figured that my best bet would be to take the treatment early in the day so that I would be ready for a reasonably early bedtime. I figured that regulating my sleep patterns during this time may play a key role in ending the attacks.
I proceeded to take these low doses of LSD every day for five days. During that time, I had only a few shadows. They honestly didn't even feel much like cluster headaches, or migraines even for that matter, because they weren't unilateral. They felt a bit dull like a sinus headache. (I don't know about anyone else, but this is usually how I feel right before a cluster headache is coming. They build up to full pain much more quickly than a migraine. I go from having a dull pain across the front of my head to a full on excruciating eye pain within about five to ten minutes.)
In 2012, the headaches never came back. I did not have a single cluster headache for the rest of the year. I felt that I had more or less found an easy and painless method of aborting episodes.
I still have complete confidence that treating with tryptamines is the best and most effective method for treating this condition. I have had great success in 2013 when compared to my previous years, although there have been some rebound headaches this year. I'm hoping that I can refine my approach for future years.
In 2013, I did not use any mushrooms in my initial week of treatment. That is the biggest difference in the way I treated. The first headache also came late in the evening, when I was almost ready for bed. I did not feel prepared for a "trip" or anything that would keep me from a decent bedtime, so I went ahead and went to bed with that headache.

Hey everyone, I've been away for a while. My cycle petered out in February and I returned to my "baseline". Thanks again to everyone who supported me during the dark times.
My "baseline" isn't plain sailing by any means. For years I've been dealing with more or less daily chronic head/face pain. It ranges from a 1-4 most days, although maybe I'll get the odd day off here and there. This "baseline" pain is mainly on the left side of my head and tends to occur under my eye, in my upper teeth, in my ear, in my neck (with autonomic symptoms too). I will get more painful exacerbations (6-8) every so often (over endurance, in heat/humidity, when my sleep is disrupted) although those never quite seem to mirror the classical cluster attacks I get on the right side of my head - they can last 24 hours, maybe more, and always ramp up slowly rather than come out of nowhere.
A few weeks ago I started to have premonitory symptoms of another more typical right sided CH cycle. Wasn't expecting it as I've typically been cycling only in the winter over the last few years. Started getting right sided "shadows". I got back on the D3 regimen and upped my melatonin dosage. For a couple of weeks I didn't get any cluster attacks, and was able to get an occipital nerve block. I still haven't had any classical cluster attacks as of writing.
Over the course of these three weeks, my left sided pain has been worse. And it feels like my symptoms yo-yo from one side of my head to the other. On any given day, I might have pain switching sides multiple times. I can often notice it on both sides at the same time. Whilst I guess I should be grateful that I'm not experiencing full blown cluster attacks, I still find coping with almost constant pain very draining. I do wonder sometimes how I manage to get by, being a father to two young children, and holding down a senior, full-time job. Life is a real struggle, although I do get some weeks/months where things aren't too bad (regular, light/moderate exercise does seem to help outside of my classic cycles). Being in pain to some extent all the time sucks the enjoyment out of life.
My neurologist in the UK is one of the most respected in cluster headache and migraine. So he supposedly knows his stuff. He's insisted for years that my left sided pain is chronic migraine, but I'm beginning to get sort of fed up insisting that it's more likely to by CH related. He says I'm ECH with chronic migraine, but I just don't understand any longer how the left sided symptoms aren't CH, albeit essentially chronic "shadows" rather than meeting the diagnostic criteria for CCH. I suffered migraine all through my childhood and those symptoms were noticeably different to what I have now. My neurologist said this is because adding CH to the mix tends to morph migraine more in the direction of CH to some extent. Does it even really matter what it is? Should the focus just be on treating it rather than obsessing over the diagnosis?

I've not been on any CH support sites for a long time. Things had been going good the last few years. Right now...well...yeah.
I've been fighting chronic CH for 19 years now. Yep, 19 years. I've been through a hell of a lot with them, close to suicide more than once. It's a bitch. I'm fortunate to have an understanding employer and a caring wife. I don't know how I could have survived over the years without the support.
I've been seeing Dr. Sachdeva in San Ramon for years, she's probably a lot of the reason I've survived this long. On top of that, she got me over to Dr. Goadsby when he was at UCSF getting their headache clinic up and running. You know your CH sucks when Dr. Goadsby tells you that you're one of his toughest cases. Unfortunately, Dr. Goadsby has moved on to other places to help other people, and the doctor at UCSF who took over CH cases since then was doing well, but he has now moved on as well, leaving those of us with CH on a waiting list.
Over a decade ago, I spent five days sitting in UCSF hospital with a tube in my arm getting DHE dosed every few hours. After that, I was titrated up on Verapamil to where things finally worked to calm my CH down, which was at 320mg 3X a day (yep, 960mg a day). I did reasonably well on it, the CH was there at a low enough level I could survive and when it started getting bad they would do a greater occipital nerve block that would calm things down for a while. Over the last few years, my heart started slowing down (thanks to hitting middle age) and I had to get weaned down on the Verapamil to 160mg 3X a day based on the EKG and review with the cardiologist I see (he's Dr. Sachdeva's husband, so they both keep an eye on me!). I've done another three visits in to UCSF for five days of DHE to quiet things down as well since then.
So now I've got my daily Verapamil, 200IU of D3 nightly, and Duloxitine for the depression that always comes with chronic pain. I use Tylenol for lower level hits, then Imitrex for the harder ones. Oxygen therapy has unfortunately never worked for me (Dr. Goadsby said sometimes that's just how it goes). When it gets really insane, my wife will run me off to the ER and they'll throw a combination of stuff into me, fortunately that happens only once every few months. The last year had been quiet for me, I think because my body was preoccupied dealing with pain from shattering a heel when I fell off a ladder. This is some basic information that I’ve been wanting to put in writing for a while.  The topics here are
doctors and diagnoses

oxygen

the D3 regimen

.
Busting (treating CH with psychedelics, potentially at sub-hallucinogenic levels) is the reason this site exists, and it is a highly effective, low-side-effect way to end CH cycles and possibly prevent future cycles. Everyone with CH should consider it.
At the end of this post
, I have attached the basic information about busting that appears on various pages in the blue heading, "New Users -- Read This First."
I know I’m leaving out a lot. This should not be considered as a substitute for more thorough exploration. Just a starting point. I am hoping that others will correct, clarify, and amplify what’s here. One way to learn more about any of these topics is to put a word or words into the search bar at the top right of any page, and be sure that “all content” is checked. There’s a ton and a half of information here.
Doctors and Diagnoses
Most general-practice physicians are clueless about CH. Not only do they generally misdiagnose it, they don’t seem to have the energy or compassion to look up proper treatments when there is a correct diagnosis. Neurologists may be slightly better, but usually not much. Your best bet for diagnosis/treatment is a headache center, particularly one associated with a university.
Of course, a doctor’s treatment options are generally limited to pharmaceuticals. More about pharma treatments later.
You should have an MRI to rule out possible other causes of your pain.
There are a few conditions that are CH “lookalikes” that should be kept in mind, particularly if standard CH treatments (or busting) aren’t working. Probably the most common of these are hemicranias (which you can look up). Hemicranias are treatable with a drug called indomethacin, which should be tried if CH treatments aren’t working. There are also conditions caused by veins in the brain, called “venous headaches,” and cervicogenic headaches, which begin in the neck but create severe head pain. But if you have CH symptoms, the greatest likelihood is that you have CH.
Oxygen
You should have oxygen from a tank/cylinder/cannister,
not
a concentrator that makes O2 from room air. You should have either a
non-rebreather
mask or the mask that is made for people with CH, the “Cluster O2 Kit,” which you can buy here:
http://www.clusterheadaches.com/ccp8/
.
The flow rate should be sufficient that the reservoir bag on your mask is always full when you are ready to inhale using a forceful breathing strategy. For some people, this is 15 liters per minute (lpm); for others it can be higher. Since medical O2 suppliers generally won’t provide a regulator that goes higher than 15 lpm, you might have to get your own regulator. More about regulators below.
You want to have at least two tanks: one large one for home

Hi Scott! Welcome to the Board. Glad that you found us!
Well, you pharmacist may know meds, but not CH. The front line med would be Verapamil, starting at about 240mg/day and working up. A Prednisone taper is usually given at the beginning for a week or so to get you some pain free time while the Verap builds up in your body Long term use of Prednisone is not good for your body and should be avoided. The Verap dose can go up to over 900mg/day for some. It needs to be monitored with dose increases. And Imitrex is given in shot form for the fastest abort for CH. Nasal beats the pills given for migraines, but is not as fast as the injection. Pills work too slow for CH and should only be given for migraines. As you have found out, you cannot get enough of it to treat all of your hits. And you can't take an injection or spray for every hit! Most will load up on Trex when out of cycle to be better prepared when they get one. It is hard on the heart and the daily use is limited to two in 24 hours. Insurance usually only approves 9 to 12 a month. Which does not begin to treat them all!!! There is a way to split the injections into 3 doses that helps a lot of people. The full dose is usually more than is needed, especially when gotten to quickly. That does up your number of hits that you can kill with it. However, Trex is a double-edged sword - it can cause rebound hits and you wind up with an escalating cycle of more and more hits.
Ch has a lovely ability to morph over time. Frequency of hits changes, dates change, etc. Sorry that you have had this happen. Nocturnal hits sort of suck big time. Often by the time you wake, it is way up there on the pain scale. You can try sleeping in a recliner with it partly reclined - keep your heart above your head. It may not prevent your hits, but you will wake sooner to deal with one. That can be a huge help!!
Another help is Energy Drinks or Shots. Caffeine helps a lot! Some feel the Taurine in the drinks makes them work better than coffee, but for those who can't take them, coffee is great too. I keep it cold in the fridge for the nights. Grab it on the way to the O2 tank and get a faster abort. By itself, with a bit of deep inhales and hard exhales of room air can sometimes kill a hit too.
Pure O2 at 15 to 25 LPM will be your best friend for aborts. Not a concentrator! You will use a non-rebreather mask, so no room air is inhaled. Basically you force out the CO2 with a hard exhale with a crunch at the end and then a big inhale, hold for a bit, and repeat. You dial your O2 up to support the breathing - don't want to wait for the bag on the mask to fill, you need it waiting for that inhale. When the pain is gone, then you do your 'post hit' breathing. Dial it down and breath normally for five minutes or so. This prevents an immediate return of the hit. If your insurance covers it, get it! If not, then we can get you set up with welding O2 to use. No doctor needed then.

I'll describe my situation and then my simple remedy...
I'm 33 and have had episodic ch attacks for nearly twenty years. Cycles last 1-3 months, remissions last from 6 months to as long as a year and a half. Because of the episodic nature I never spent enough time trying to figure out what was going on, so I just assumed they were migraines. But did people really cry and bang their fists on the floor over migraines? After each cycle ended I would think they were gone forever. Just wishful thinking. I didn't learn of cluster headaches until I was 30. As soon as I read about them it was obvious what I had.
My ch pain is always behind my left eye and radiates to the top of my head (left side) and down the left side of my neck. Cycles tend to start in late winter/early spring. Headaches start in the morning as early as 2:00 AM but usually around 9:00 AM. This last cycle has been different from others in that the attack lasts longer, up to six hours. The attacks used to suddenly end after an hour but this time they build up for an hour and maintain intensity for a couple hours and then slowly wind down, completely exhausting me and ruining my day.
During an attack I have a need to heat up my whole body either in a tub or preferably in front of a space heater where I'll wrap a blanket around me and the heater. My nervous system seems to be out of whack making me feel slightly nauseous with temperature fluctuations.
My solution:
The last thing I do before bed is drink a full glass of water and take a 1 mg melatonin tablet. That's it. The most important thing is the water. I drink steam distilled water, but that's my choice, I don't like or trust tap water. I know this sounds kinda ridiculous but I don't normally drink a lot of water unless I'm in the hot sun and sweating a lot. Maybe that's why I don't get the ch attacks in the summer. I drink a lot more water in the summer.
It's possible that some of us simply have a problem hydrating ourselves. I prefer to drink coffee during the cooler months. If I do things my way I'll literally only drink a half glass of straight water a day during the winter, getting my fluids from other beverages and fruit.
I know this water before bed thing is working because I went a few days without an attack and assumed I was in remission. I went to bed without water and spent the next morning in pain. After another 4 days or so I made the same mistake and got back on track with the water. I take the Melatonin about every other night. Besides giving me a great nights sleep it helps to suppress the pain if I don't have enough water and end up with a ch headache.
I'm sure a lot of you drink plenty of water and will find this of no use, but if you happen to be like me and have to force yourself to drink water because you aren't craving it, then maybe this will help.

Hey StayPositive,
Great questions.  CH is like like crap.  It happens.   It happens even when on an effective preventative treatment protocol that has kept you CH pain free in the past.  The single biggest speed bump CHers face on the anti-inflammatory regimen CH and MH preventative treatment protocol, is an immune system response to an allergen or allergens in the air or food we eat.  These allergic reactions can be subclinical with no outward or obvious symptoms.  This happens even when CHers have had lab assays that indicate they've elevated their serum 25(OH)D3 up between 80 to 100 ng/mL (200 to 250 nmol/L) or higher.
An allergen insult usually results in the immune system's Mast Cells degranulating releasing a cocktail of inflammatory mediators with histamine being the prime offender as illustrated in the following graphic of a Mast Cell.
Histamine to a CHer is like Kryptonite to Superman - Bad News as it overpowers CH preventatives.  Once the trigeminovascular system is flooded with histamine, none of the Standards of Care CH preventatives are effective and that includes MM and vitamin D3.  Histamine triggers neurons and glia in the trigeminal ganglia to express (release) Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptides (CGRP) and other neuroactive peptides that result in neurogenic inflammation and the pain we know as CH.
In the past, I've suggested CHers following the anti-inflammatory regimen treatment protocol take a first-generation antihistamine like Benadryl (Diphenhydramine HCL) as it blocks the histamine H1 receptors in neurons and glia in the trigeminal ganglia and this helps prevent the expression of CGRP.  The problem with Diphenhydramine is the side effects like drowsiness and a wired feeling.  In February of this year I began suggesting a cocktail of natural supplements with antihistamine properties.  It includes 3 grams/day each of Turmeric (Curcumin), Resveratrol, Quercetin and Omega-3 fish oil.  This cocktail also includes 200 mg.day selenium, 8 grams/day vitamin C and 10 mg/day Melatonin at bedtime..  I buy the vitamin C in 1 Kg bulk powder bags and stir two level teaspoon measures in 8 oz of water and take sips all day until its gone by bedtime.  I call this concoction the Antihistamine Full Monty.  It has ho real side effects.
I realize this is a lot of supplements to take, but the results should be worth it.  Most CHers who have tried the Antihistamine Full Monty report a pain free response in less than a week after starting it.  It worked that way for me and it has kept me CH pain free through the heavy Spring pollen season where I've typically had to increase my vitamin D3 intake to a high as 30,000 IU/day.  Moreover, once you've achieved a CH pain free response for at least a week, you can start tapering the dose of the Turmeric (Curcumin), Resveratrol, Quercetin and Omega-3 fish oil by one gram per week and discontinue the Selenium and Melatonin.  The vitamin C is so important for good health, it's prudent to keep taking at least 3

Why Are Cluster Headaches Affected by the Seasons?
Cluster headaches are the most common form of the trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias. This fascinating neurological disorder causes bouts of extreme, stabbing pain centered around the eye and temple that peak within a few minutes. They are nicknamed “suicide headaches” because of their severity. One characteristic that makes this condition so interesting is that the attacks tend to occur at the same time every day.
These attacks can happen at the same time each day, up to 8 times a day or as few as once every other day. Some clusterheads can set their watch by their attacks, and have anxiety or fear leading up to those times. What’s more, attacks frequently occur at night when transitioning between rapid eye movement (REM) sleep and another sleep stage, which robs clusterheads of their rest and, often, sanity.
Episodic patients typically go into a cycle of attacks when the seasons change, particularly around the solstices when the longest and shortest days of the year occur and also around the equinoxes when we change our clocks for daylight savings time.
Why cluster headaches are linked to seasonal changes is likely due to the amount of daylight that occurs. Seasonal shifts make nights shorter and days longer or vice versa which can disrupt our sleep cycles leading to attacks.
March 21st is the European day for cluster headache awareness because of the spring equinox when “episodics” are more likely to experience attacks. Cluster headache patients, or “clusterheads,” are more likely to go into an episodic cycle (chronic patients may go into a high cycle) in the spring and fall as well as January and February when the days are shortest or July and August when the days are longest. Because of the link between cluster headaches and the seasons, the disorder can be mistaken for allergies or sinusitis.
The link between cluster headaches and seasons, time of day, and sleep cycles points researchers to the hypothalamus—a small area of the brain that regulates the body’s biological clock, hormones, and circadian rhythms.
How Your Brain Changes with the Seasons
The human body can be a working piece of art that usually functions in a healthy, balanced manner effortlessly, but dysfunction in a part of the brain the size of an almond called the hypothalamus is enough to create a cascading impact on the entire body. 14 hours ago, CHfather said:
Is the candesartan for the CH and/or migraine?  If so, is there a reason why it's not verapamil instead?
A very good question! I started on Candesartan in 2018 and have been on it ever since. At roughly the same time, I also started on Melatonin. In both cases, the primary reason for prescription was to treat my chronic head/face pain, on the assumption that it is a manifestation of chronic migraine. It was considered a sensible choice on the basis that both drugs, especially melatonin, have seen some benefits in CH too. So there was a theory that, if both helped my chronic migraine pain, there could be the added benefit that my ECH would be kept at bay too. I have previously challenged my neuro about verapamil and he was against it on the basis that it can often make migraine worse (he said he'd only use verapamil for me to treat ECH during a cycle - which I've never needed to do as nerve blocks, oxygen and triptans have sufficed in the past). I'm not particularly satisfied with that position given that there's a reasonable chance that my chronic symptoms could well be CH related, and not migraine. To be fair, I definitely saw a noticeable general improvement after starting candesartan and melatonin, but it's obviously not helpful that I started them so close together because it's not possible to determine the individual effects.

I'll give an in depth review of mescaline below including dosage, frequency, administration, and which particular source I've obtained it from "Plant Source"!
Mescaline
is a naturally-occurring psychedelic alkaloid of the phenethylamine class. It shares properties of some major hallucinogens and that of the drug class phenethylamine. Some major drugs that are listed as phenethylamines are Dopamine, MDMA, Adrenaline, Amphetamines, and Norepinephrine. The chemical properties of mescaline really do intrigue me when looking into the main causes of a CH attack. It has been said that irregularity of serotonin, dopamine, and melatonin can play a vital role in finding the trigger behind these attacks. I believe mescaline is the best solution because it takes the key to opening the receptor up for it to fool serotonin and the chemical properties to make it last. This is due to the drugs phenethylamine effects...increased heart rate increased oxygen overall, increased energy, and sometimes MDMA like effects. Mescaline seems to linger in the system afterward causing tolerance buildup.Ok now for my trials.

I personally recommend Peruvian Torch for its potency and consistency. I first experimented with San pedro that I had found in the desert myself, it was impotent and a waste of time, I suggest ordering online which is 100% Legal and or growing it yourself once again 100% legal. Ok now the most annoying thing about Peruvian torch is that batches vary in potency, A usually aim for a strong to medium trip, which is more then enough for the benefits. This ranges in dry weight of Peruvian Torch cacti by anywhere from 20g-50g. I have had really potent batches that made me trip only after ingesting 20g and then again some that took 45g+. Obviously for sake of your stomach you should aim for the most potent product you can obtain. 20g activity is pretty rare so my best dosage recommendation would be a safe 35g.
